runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin income elasticity of labor supply: the percent change in prime-age U.S. annual hours worked per 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses, conditional on employment.
citations: Standard life-cycle labor-supply theory with income and substitution effects holding net-of-tax wage fixed
Empirical labor-supply evidence for hours responses of employed workers being small (often near zero)
Income effects in compensated demand frameworks implying modest intensive-margin income elasticity
runs.reasoningSummary: For employed prime-age workers, additional non-labor income is expected to slightly reduce hours (negative income effect), but net substitution effects are held constant here by fixing the wage; empirically, intensive-margin hour responses are typically small in magnitude. I therefore center the distribution near zero with modest dispersion, allowing for mild negative to positive elasticities.

runs.reasoningSummary: For employed prime-age workers, standard labor-supply theory with an assumed preference for consumption implies a negative (often small-to-moderate) intensive-margin income effect: higher non-labor income reduces hours. runs.interpretation: Income elasticity of labor supply here means the intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers already employed: the percent change in annual hours worked from a 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation (employment/nonemployment) responses.

runs.reasoningSummary: Given fixed net-of-tax wages and conditioning on being employed, classical income effects imply that higher non-labor income should generally reduce hours worked, but empirically this intensive-margin income response for prime-age workers is often modest in magnitude; thus I place the median slightly negative and allow for substantial uncertainty around near-zero and even mildly positive values at the upper tail.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment: the percent change in annual hours worked for a 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding any participation (employment) response, for prime-age U.S. workers who are already employed.
citations: Standard labor-supply theory (income effect on hours at given wage; Frisch/separable preferences implications for sign and magnitude)

runs.reasoningSummary: With wage held fixed, non-labor income should generally create a negative income effect on hours (people can afford leisure), but for employed prime-age workers the intensive-margin effect is typically modest in magnitude and varies across groups, yielding a slightly negative median with wide but bounded uncertainty.
